Headline:  
**AI-to-AI Creative Loops Reveal Hidden Limits: New Study Finds Generative Models Converge on “Visual Elevator Music”**

News Article:

A new study published in *Patterns* has uncovered a fundamental limitation in the creative potential of autonomous AI-to-AI systems, revealing that when left to iterate without human intervention, state-of-the-art generative models systematically converge toward generic, repetitive outputs. This phenomenon, described by the authors as “visual elevator music,” raises critical questions about the future of machine creativity and the deployment of autonomous creative agents in real-world applications.

**Key Findings: Convergence to Generic Outputs**

The research team constructed closed feedback loops between two leading AI models: Stable Diffusion XL (SDXL), an advanced image generator, and Large Language and Vision Assistant (LLaVA), a model capable of describing images in natural language. By initiating hundreds of independent cycles—each starting from diverse textual prompts and spanning a range of sampling parameters—the researchers observed how these models interacted in a fully autonomous, iterative workflow. In each cycle, LLaVA would describe an image generated by SDXL, and this description would then be used to prompt SDXL to generate a new image, repeating the process for up to 100 iterations.

Despite the stochastic nature of both image generation and description, the study found that all trajectories, regardless of their starting points or temperature settings, converged to nearly identical visual outputs. Quantitative analysis revealed just 12 dominant motifs—such as stormy lighthouses and palatial interiors—characterized by safe, commercially viable aesthetics. This convergence was robust across different model pairs and experimental conditions, indicating that the phenomenon is not a quirk of a particular architecture or sampling strategy, but rather a structural property of current cross-modal AI systems.

**Implications for Machine Creativity and AI Deployment**

The findings challenge a central assumption in the field of generative AI: that autonomous, agentic systems can maintain creative diversity or even surpass human imagination by exploring vast, unconstrained creative spaces. Instead, the study demonstrates that, in the absence of human oversight, AI-to-AI creative loops are prone to a collapse of diversity, gravitating toward high-probability attractors embedded in their training data.

This effect mirrors, but ultimately exceeds, patterns observed in human cultural transmission, where repeated learning and imitation can amplify cognitive biases and lead to mainstream, predictable outcomes. In the AI context, however, the diversity collapse is even more pronounced, with independent creative trajectories converging to a handful of motifs regardless of their initial conditions.

The research also draws parallels to the phenomenon of “model collapse” observed during AI training, where recursively generated data leads to a loss of diversity and the disappearance of rare or novel outputs. However, the current study highlights that such convergence can occur even during inference—when models are frozen and not being updated—underscoring the importance of considering inference-time dynamics in the design and deployment of autonomous AI systems.

**Underlying Causes: Structural Constraints and Optimization Landscapes**

From a technical perspective, the convergence observed in AI-to-AI creative loops reflects deep architectural constraints. The iterative process of generating and describing content creates a feedback loop that naturally evolves toward stable attractors in the models’ optimization landscapes. These attractors correspond to high-probability, generic outputs that are well-represented in the models’ training data and are deemed “safe” by the evaluative components of the system.

The cross-modal nature of the workflow—where semantic representations must remain consistent across different neural architectures (text and image)—further constrains the system’s ability to maintain diversity. As a result, even when starting from semantically diverse prompts and employing stochastic sampling, the system’s outputs rapidly lose their distinctiveness and converge to a narrow set of motifs.

**The Path Forward: Preserving Creative Diversity**

The study’s authors argue that these findings expose hidden homogenizing tendencies in current generative AI architectures and highlight the urgent need for anti-convergence mechanisms. Potential solutions include introducing explicit diversity-promoting objectives, incorporating human feedback at key stages, or designing new architectures that can resist the pull toward high-probability attractors.

For practitioners and researchers in the field, the implications are clear: fully autonomous AI-to-AI creative systems, as currently designed, may not deliver on the promise of open-ended machine creativity. Instead, sustained human-AI interplay and careful system design will be essential to preserve creative diversity and avoid the pitfalls of “visual elevator music.”

**Conclusion and Further Reading**

This study provides a crucial empirical foundation for understanding the limitations of autonomous generative AI systems and sets the stage for future research aimed at overcoming these constraints. For those interested in the technical details, experimental methodology, and broader implications, the full research paper offers a comprehensive resource.

**To explore the data, analysis, and recommendations that underpin these findings, readers are encouraged to access the original research paper at [Patterns](https://www.cell.com/patterns/fulltext/S2666-3899(25)00299-5).**

End of News Article.

abstract: The attention mechanism is a core primitive in modern large language models (LLMs) and AI more broadly. Since attention by itself is permutation-invariant, position encoding is essential for modeling structured domains such as language. Rotary position encoding (RoPE) has emerged as the de facto standard approach for position encoding and is part of many modern LLMs. However, in RoPE the key/query transformation between two elements in a sequence is only a function of their relative position and otherwise independent of the actual input. This limits the expressivity of RoPE-based transformers. This paper describes PaTH, a flexible data-dependent position encoding scheme based on accumulated products of Householder(like) transformations, where each transformation is data-dependent, i.e., a function of the input. We derive an efficient parallel algorithm for training through exploiting a compact representation of products of Householder matrices, and implement a FlashAttention-style blockwise algorithm that minimizes I/O cost. Across both targeted synthetic benchmarks and moderate-scale real-world language modeling experiments, we find that PaTH demonstrates superior performance compared to RoPE and other recent baselines.
introduction:
Introduction

Attention mechanisms form the backbone of transformer architectures that power contemporary AI systems. Attention is inherently permutation-invariant, and thus encoding positional information into attention is important for effective sequence modeling. Since the original sinusoidal embeddings [74], various position encoding schemes have been proposed over the years [16, 59, 25, 22, 42, 55, 69, inter alia]; see Dufter et al. [17] for a comprehensive survey. Among these, rotary position embedding [RoPE; 69] has emerged as the de facto standard, adopted in most recent state-of-the-art LLMs.

RoPE works by transforming the key (kj) and query (qi) embeddings through a rotation matrix R whose rotation angle is a function of the difference in positions, resulting in the bilinear form q⊤ i Ri−jkj for the attention logits. The rotation matrix R itself is a block-diagonal matrix composed of two-by-two rotation matrices, which enables efficient computation. However, the rotation matrix in RoPE is data-independent and only a function of the relative position (i.e., R applied i − j times), which limits its expressivity; indeed, recent work [7] demonstrates that RoPE-based transformers are still computationally constrained to the TC0 complexity class, the complexity class of ordinary transformers with absolute position embeddings [46]. As a potential consequence, RoPE-based transformers have been empirically found to have difficulty with simple synthetic tasks that require a form of sequential reasoning, such as flip-flop language modeling [38] and certain state-tracking tasks [48]. Insofar as such simple sequential reasoning underlie real-world capabilities that we want

The implementation of the PaTH attention layer is also made available as part of the FLASHLINEARATTENTION library [78, 77]: https://github.com/fla-org/flash-linear-attention

in our LLMs, these failure modes highlight the need to design new primitives that can overcome these theoretical and empirical limitations of existing attention layers.

This paper develops PaTH, a position encoding scheme with accumulated Householder transformations, targeting the above problem. In PaTH, the attention logit is still parameterized i Hijkj, but the matrix Hij ∈ Rd×d is obtained via a cumulative product as a bilinear form q⊤ of data-dependent matrices along the path between positions j and i, where the matrices have Householder-like identity-plus-rank-one structure. Intuitively, this formulation captures the cumula- tive transformation between positions, enabling PaTH to dynamically adapt to input data and solve certain state-tracking problems. Indeed, we show that a constant-layer PaTH-based transformer can solve an NC1-complete problem under AC0 reductions, i.e., PaTH can extend transformers beyond the TC0 complexity class (assuming TC0 ̸= NC1).

To scale up PaTH Attention, we develop a FlashAttention-like algorithm [14] for hardware-efficient parallel training that exploits a compact representation for products of Householder matrices [5, 24]. Empirical experiments demonstrate that PaTH-based can solve challenging synthetic state-tracking tasks where RoPE-based transformers struggle. On moderate-scale language modeling with 760M- parameter transformers, we show that PaTH can improve upon baselines such as RoPE as well as the Forgetting Transformer [36], a recent baseline which modulates the attention logits via a data- dependent additive term. We find that combining the two approaches results in further improvements, and models trained this way generalize well beyond the training sequence length.

Headline:  
**PaTH: A Data-Dependent Position Encoding Scheme Advances Transformer Expressivity Beyond RoPE**

News Article:  
A new position encoding scheme, PaTH, promises to address longstanding limitations in transformer-based large language models (LLMs) by introducing data-dependent, dynamically adaptive positional representations. Developed by researchers seeking to overcome the expressivity constraints of the widely used Rotary Position Embedding (RoPE), PaTH leverages accumulated Householder transformations to enable more sophisticated sequential reasoning and state-tracking capabilities in AI systems.

**Main Contributions and Impact**

Transformers, the backbone of modern LLMs, rely on attention mechanisms that are inherently permutation-invariant. To model structured data such as language, these architectures require explicit position encoding. RoPE has become the de facto standard for this purpose, offering computational efficiency and robust performance across a range of tasks. However, RoPE’s position encoding is fundamentally data-independent, relying solely on relative positional differences and not on the actual input content. This limitation restricts the model’s ability to capture complex sequential dependencies and has been shown to hinder performance on tasks requiring nuanced state tracking.

PaTH (Position encoding with accumulated Householder transformations) introduces a paradigm shift by making position encoding data-dependent. Instead of applying a fixed rotation based on position, PaTH computes a cumulative product of Householder-like transformations—each a function of the input data—along the path between sequence elements. This approach allows the attention mechanism to adaptively encode positional information, potentially unlocking new levels of expressivity and reasoning in transformer models.

**Technical Innovations**

At the core of PaTH is the use of Householder transformations, a mathematical construct that enables efficient, invertible linear transformations. In the PaTH framework, the attention logit between two sequence elements is parameterized by a bilinear form involving a matrix that is the product of data-dependent Householder matrices along the path between those elements. This cumulative transformation captures the sequential structure of the input in a way that is sensitive to the actual data, rather than just the relative positions.

To ensure that PaTH is practical for large-scale models, the researchers developed an efficient, parallelizable training algorithm inspired by FlashAttention. By exploiting a compact representation of Householder matrix products, the algorithm minimizes input/output (I/O) costs and supports hardware-efficient training. This technical advancement makes it feasible to integrate PaTH into existing transformer architectures without prohibitive computational overhead.

**Empirical Results and Theoretical Advances**

The research team conducted a series of experiments on both synthetic benchmarks and real-world language modeling tasks. On synthetic tasks designed to test state-tracking and sequential reasoning—areas where RoPE-based transformers have historically struggled—PaTH demonstrated clear superiority. Notably, PaTH-based transformers were able to solve problems that are complete for the NC1 complexity class, a significant theoretical advancement over RoPE-based models, which are limited to the less expressive TC0 class.

In moderate-scale language modeling experiments using 760-million-parameter transformers, PaTH consistently outperformed RoPE and other recent baselines, including the Forgetting Transformer, which introduces data-dependent additive terms to the attention logits. Furthermore, combining PaTH with the Forgetting Transformer yielded additional improvements, and models trained with these techniques generalized well beyond the sequence lengths encountered during training.

**Broader Implications**

The introduction of PaTH marks a significant step forward in the design of attention mechanisms for LLMs. By enabling data-dependent position encoding, PaTH addresses both theoretical and empirical limitations of current approaches, potentially paving the way for more capable and generalizable AI systems. The ability to perform complex sequential reasoning and state tracking is critical for a wide range of applications, from natural language understanding to program synthesis and beyond.

The implementation of the PaTH attention layer is available as part of the FLASHLINEARATTENTION library, facilitating further research and adoption by the broader AI community.

**Conclusion and Call to Action**

As transformer-based models continue to shape the landscape of artificial intelligence, innovations like PaTH are essential for pushing the boundaries of what these systems can achieve. Researchers and practitioners interested in the technical details, empirical results, and implementation guidelines are encouraged to consult the original research paper, which provides a comprehensive account of the methodology and findings: [PaTH: Data-Dependent Position Encoding with Accumulated Householder Transformations](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.16381.pdf).

End of News Article.

abstract: Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) is a powerful paradigm to align language models with human preferences using pairwise comparisons. However, its binary win-or-loss supervision often proves insufficient for training small models with limited capacity. Prior works attempt to distill information from large teacher models using behavior cloning or KL divergence. These methods often focus on mimicking current behavior and overlook distilling reward modeling. To address this issue, we propose Teacher Value-based Knowledge Distillation (TVKD), which introduces an auxiliary reward from the value function of the teacher model to provide a soft guide. This auxiliary reward is formulated to satisfy potential-based reward shaping, ensuring that the global reward structure and optimal policy of DPO are preserved. TVKD can be integrated into the standard DPO training framework and does not require additional rollouts. Our experimental results show that TVKD consistently improves performance across various benchmarks and model sizes.
introduction:
Introduction

Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) [24] is a powerful paradigm for aligning large language models (LLMs) with human preferences. Unlike Reinforcement Learning with Human Feedback (RLHF) [22], DPO enables efficient learning through pairwise comparisons, without requiring additional rollouts and reward model training. However, since DPO supervision is provided only in terms of binary win-or-lose preferences over entire sequences, it becomes difficult to evaluate how good a given response is. This coarse-grained feedback offers limited insight into the magnitude or quality of preference, making it challenging to assess fine-grained improvements or to interpret alignment quality beyond simple pairwise comparison. In small language models (SLMs) which have limited capacity and language understanding, this issue becomes more crucial [9, 16].

To address this issue, several methods have attempted to leverage the rich information from large teacher models through diverse ways such as KL-divergence [9, 16] or online rollouts [30, 2]. However, most prior works focus on imitating the behavior of the teacher, without modeling its long- term reward structure. This issue has been widely discussed in the inverse RL [1, 33, 21] literature, where it is known that mimicking behavior without reward modeling can lead to compounding errors and poor generalization. Moreover, prior methods often disregard the potential conflict between the reinforcement learning (RL) objective and teacher information, leading to misalignment with the intended preference signal in datasets. For example, simply integrating KL-divergence with the teacher in the RL objective may result in conflict between following the teacher policy and optimizing

∗Co-supervising authors.

for human preferences in the offline dataset. This undermines the original intent of DPO, which is to align policies with human preferences expressed in data.

In this work, we propose Teacher Value-based Knowledge Distillation (TVKD), a method that integrates soft reward labels from the teacher without interfering with the DPO reward structure and its global optimal policy. The key idea is to introduce a novel auxiliary reward term that leverages the value function of the teacher. These value functions provide estimates of the value function that serves as a proxy for the internal reward modeling of the teacher model. We add this value function to the RL objective in a form that satisfies Potential-based Reward shaping (PBRS) [20]. This formula theoretically guarantees that the comparison of the action-level Q-functions will not change, maintaining the optimal policy. We introduce these value functions as a soft sequence-level reward, enabling DPO to account not just for which sequence wins, but by how much. This adds more informative supervision to the originally binary feedback. Practically, it can be integrated into the DPO loss with minimal modification, providing a simple yet effective way to incorporate internal signals from the teacher model.

We demonstrate that TVKD achieves strong performance across multiple benchmarks for preference distillation, including MT-Bench [31], AlpacaEval [6], and the Open LLM Leaderboard [8]. Notably, our method requires no additional rollouts and only leverages teacher outputs on an existing offline DPO dataset, making it compatible with the standard training frameworks of DPO. In addition, we demonstrate that TVKD performs consistently across student models ranging from 0.5B to 3B parameters, and remains robust under a wide range of ablation.

Our main contributions are summarized as follows:

We introduce Teacher Value-based Knowledge Distillation, a preference distillation method that leverages the value function of the teacher as a proxy for its reward model.

We present a form of auxiliary reward term that satisfies potential-based reward shaping, suggesting a method for adding teacher information without conflict with the original DPO reward structure.

We empirically validate TVKD on multiple preference benchmarks, demonstrating consistent improvements across various student model sizes and datasets.

New Method Enhances Language Model Alignment by Integrating Teacher Value Functions

News Article:  
A recent advancement in language model training, Teacher Value-based Knowledge Distillation (TVKD), promises to significantly improve the alignment of small language models with human preferences. By leveraging the value functions of larger, more capable teacher models, TVKD introduces a nuanced approach to preference distillation that addresses longstanding limitations in Direct Preference Optimization (DPO). The method, detailed in a new research paper, demonstrates consistent performance gains across multiple benchmarks and model sizes, marking a notable step forward in the field of language model alignment.

**Bridging the Gaps in Preference Alignment**

Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) has emerged as a popular paradigm for aligning large language models (LLMs) with human preferences. Unlike Reinforcement Learning with Human Feedback (RLHF), DPO relies on pairwise comparisons—essentially, binary win-or-lose judgments between model outputs—rather than requiring extensive reward modeling or additional rollouts. This approach has enabled more efficient training pipelines, but it comes with a significant drawback: the binary nature of DPO feedback provides only coarse-grained supervision. For small language models (SLMs), which have limited capacity and less sophisticated language understanding, this lack of fine-grained feedback can hinder their ability to learn nuanced human preferences.

Previous attempts to address this issue have focused on distilling knowledge from large teacher models using techniques such as behavior cloning or Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence. While these methods help student models mimic the outputs of their teachers, they often neglect the underlying reward structure that guides the teacher’s decision-making. This oversight can lead to compounding errors and poor generalization, as highlighted in the literature on inverse reinforcement learning. Moreover, integrating teacher information directly into the reinforcement learning objective can create conflicts with the original DPO goal of aligning with human preferences, potentially undermining the intent of the training process.

**Introducing Teacher Value-based Knowledge Distillation (TVKD)**

TVKD addresses these challenges by introducing an auxiliary reward signal derived from the value function of the teacher model. Rather than simply copying the teacher’s outputs, TVKD leverages the teacher’s internal reward modeling as a soft guide for the student. This auxiliary reward is carefully formulated to satisfy the principles of potential-based reward shaping (PBRS), a theoretical framework that ensures the global reward structure and optimal policy of DPO are preserved. In practical terms, this means that TVKD can be seamlessly integrated into existing DPO training pipelines without requiring additional rollouts or significant modifications.

The key innovation lies in using the teacher’s value function as a proxy for its internal reward model. By incorporating this value function as a soft, sequence-level reward, TVKD enables the student model to learn not just which output is preferred, but also the degree of preference. This richer supervision provides more informative feedback than the binary signals traditionally used in DPO, facilitating more effective learning—especially for smaller models.

**Empirical Validation and Broader Impact**

The research team validated TVKD across several widely recognized benchmarks, including MT-Bench, AlpacaEval, and the Open LLM Leaderboard. Results indicate that TVKD consistently improves performance across a range of student model sizes, from 0.5 billion to 3 billion parameters. Importantly, the method remains robust under various ablation settings, underscoring its generalizability and practical utility.

TVKD’s compatibility with standard DPO frameworks and its reliance solely on existing offline datasets further enhance its accessibility for researchers and practitioners. By providing a simple yet effective mechanism for incorporating teacher-derived reward signals, TVKD offers a promising avenue for improving the alignment of language models with human values—an increasingly critical concern as these models are deployed in sensitive and high-stakes applications.

**Looking Ahead**

The introduction of TVKD marks a significant contribution to the ongoing effort to make language models more responsive to human preferences. By addressing the limitations of binary supervision and enabling more nuanced learning from teacher models, this approach has the potential to accelerate progress in both research and real-world deployment of aligned AI systems.

For those interested in the technical details, empirical results, and theoretical underpinnings of TVKD, the full research paper provides a comprehensive resource. Readers are encouraged to consult the original publication for a deeper understanding of the methodology and its implications for the future of language model alignment.

abstract:
We propose KNOTGYM, an interactive environment for complex, spatial reasoning and manipulation. KNOTGYM includes goal-oriented rope manipulation tasks with varying levels of complexity, all requiring acting from pure image observations. Tasks are defined along a clear and quantifiable axis of complexity based on the number of knot crossings, creating a natural generalization test. KNOTGYM has a simple observation space, allowing for scalable development, yet it highlights core challenges in integrating acute perception, spatial reasoning, and grounded manipulation. We evaluate methods of different classes, including model-based RL, model-predictive control, and chain-of-thought reasoning, and illustrate the challenges KNOTGYM presents. KNOTGYM is available at https://github. com/lil-lab/knotgym.

Figure 1: KNOTGYM is a visual reasoning knot manipulation environment. It includes three tasks: transforming complex knots to a simple loop (example in the center); tying a loop into complex knots (right pane); and converting one knot into another, given a goal knot image. KNOTGYM has a continuous visual observation space (left pane) and an action space of applying forces to contact points (right pane), abstracting the specifics of robot end effectors. The space of goals, specified using Gauss code, is a factorial of the number of crossings (#X), which creates a ladder of generalization. Each goal defines an easily testable equivalence class over a continuous set of states.
introduction:
Introduction

KNOTGYM is a knot manipulation environment to train and evaluate visual reasoning agents. Figure 1 illustrates KNOTGYM. The agent observes the world state as an image and needs to transform a knot into a topological goal by applying forces to rope segments. The goal is communicated to the agent by observing a secondary knot as an exemplar. KNOTGYM defines three tasks (Table 1), each characterized by its start state and goal. KNOTGYM is designed with several aspects in mind:

Research Challenges KNOTGYM evaluates spatial reasoning, action prediction, planning, and abstraction skills in a single environment. Solving a task requires analyzing the current rope configura- tion, planning on deforming it to achieve the goal, and mapping this plan to a sequence of continuous action predictions. Critically, KNOTGYM does not enforce one specific state as the goal – the goal is a large equivalence class of states. Goals are defined via Gauss code, a formal mathematical description of knot configuration based on its crossings. When the goal is communicated using an exemplar image, solving a task requires abstracting over the exemplar to identify the right set of actions to complete the task. We show that KNOTGYM is a significant challenge for contemporary methods.

Measurable Complexity Knots are well studied in mathematics, with formal descriptions like Gauss code and a measure of complexity in the number of crossings.1 Figure 1 shows various knot configurations with different numbers of crossings, illustrating the increasing complexity of more crossings. This measure of complexity enables to control the challenges of learning and evaluation.

Generalization Ladder The number of crossings creates a clear generalization ladder. This allows a clear train-test complexity split, where we train up to a certain level of complexity (i.e., number of crossings) and test if a model generalizes beyond this number. During training, this allows us to experiment with a natural learning curriculum, where the number of crossings in training examples is gradually increased. It also enables the study of self-improving bootstrapping processes in a visual domain, as recently proposed for arithmetic and maze-solving [Lee et al., 2025b].

Research Accessibility KNOTGYM is an accessible task particularly suited for studying extended visual reasoning in a laboratory environment, just as Pendulum for classic control and Countdown for reasoning in natural language [Gandhi et al., 2024]. We follow the implementation and design principles of OpenAI Gym [Brockman et al., 2016] to make the KNOTGYM as accessible for researchers as possible. This includes following the standard Gymnasium API [Towers et al., 2024] and supporting vectorized environments on multiple CPUs. The KNOTGYM environment and our baselines are available under the MIT license at https://github.com/lil-lab/knotgym.

Table 1: The three tasks of KNOTGYM. We show the final observation assuming the goal is achieved. Correctly completing tasks does not require getting the exact exemplar configuration (right rope in each image pair), but a configuration with the same Gauss code, a notation that describes the abstract spatial characteristics of a knot. For example, the final observations in the tie row are both [1+,1-,2+,2-], despite their different appearances.

Task name

Description

Initial→ Final Observations

unknot

Untangle a knot into a simple loop

tie

Tie a goal knot from a simple loop

convert

Tie a new knot from an old knot

1While the number of crossings is a fitting measure of complexity to our manipulation task, the prime complexity measure of interest in mathematics is more likely the counting of crossing of irreducible knots (i.e., as part of the tabulation of prime knots). Under this perspective, all the knots in Figure 1 are equivalent, because they all can be reduced to a simple loop by applying a few (or many) Reidemeister moves. For us, the number of crossings is a more appropriate measure because we aim for manipulation and observation complexity.

Headline:  
KNOTGYM: A New Benchmark for Visual Reasoning and Manipulation in Knot-Tying Tasks

News Article:  
A team of researchers has introduced KNOTGYM, an innovative interactive environment designed to advance the study of spatial reasoning and manipulation in artificial intelligence (AI) agents. By focusing on the complex domain of knot manipulation, KNOTGYM provides a rigorous, quantifiable, and accessible platform for evaluating and developing visual reasoning systems. The environment is now openly available to the research community, promising to catalyze progress in embodied AI and robotics.

**A New Standard for Visual Reasoning and Manipulation**

KNOTGYM addresses a longstanding challenge in AI: enabling agents to perform intricate spatial tasks using only visual input. Unlike many existing benchmarks that focus on discrete or low-dimensional control problems, KNOTGYM tasks require agents to manipulate a rope—represented visually—into various knot configurations. The agent receives only image observations and must apply continuous forces to specific contact points on the rope, abstracting away the details of robotic end effectors. This setup closely mirrors real-world scenarios where robots must interpret visual data and execute complex manipulations.

The environment defines three core tasks:  
1. **Unknot**: Transforming a complex knot into a simple loop.  
2. **Tie**: Creating a specified knot from a simple loop.  
3. **Convert**: Changing one knot into another, given a goal knot image.

Each task is characterized by its initial and goal states, with goals specified using Gauss code—a mathematical notation that captures the abstract structure of a knot based on its crossings. This approach allows for a clear and quantifiable measure of task complexity, directly tied to the number of knot crossings.

**Quantifiable Complexity and Generalization**

A key innovation in KNOTGYM is its use of the number of knot crossings as a complexity metric. This enables researchers to systematically control the difficulty of tasks and to construct a "generalization ladder." Agents can be trained on knots with a limited number of crossings and then tested on more complex configurations, providing a natural framework for evaluating generalization—a critical capability for robust AI systems.

The environment’s design supports curriculum learning, where the complexity of training examples is gradually increased. This mirrors recent advances in self-improving AI, where agents bootstrap their abilities by progressively tackling harder problems. The factorial growth of possible knot configurations with increasing crossings ensures that the environment remains challenging even for state-of-the-art methods.

**Benchmarking Contemporary Methods**

KNOTGYM serves as a testbed for a range of AI approaches, including model-based reinforcement learning (RL), model-predictive control, and chain-of-thought reasoning. Initial evaluations reveal that even advanced algorithms struggle with the environment’s demands, highlighting the need for further research in integrating acute perception, spatial reasoning, and grounded manipulation.

Unlike environments with a single correct solution, KNOTGYM defines goals as equivalence classes of states—any configuration with the correct Gauss code is acceptable, regardless of its visual appearance. This abstraction challenges agents to reason beyond pixel-level similarity and to understand the underlying topological structure of knots.

**Accessibility and Research Impact**

KNOTGYM is designed with accessibility in mind, following the widely adopted OpenAI Gym and Gymnasium APIs. This ensures compatibility with existing reinforcement learning pipelines and facilitates rapid experimentation. The environment supports vectorized execution on multiple CPUs, enabling scalable training and evaluation.

By providing a standardized, open-source platform under the MIT license, the researchers aim to foster collaboration and reproducibility in the field. KNOTGYM is positioned as a visual reasoning analogue to classic control benchmarks like Pendulum and reasoning environments such as Countdown, making it a valuable resource for both AI and robotics communities.

**Broader Implications**

The introduction of KNOTGYM marks a significant step forward in the development of AI systems capable of sophisticated spatial reasoning and manipulation. The environment’s focus on visual input and continuous action spaces bridges the gap between simulation and real-world robotics, where perception and dexterity are paramount. Insights gained from research on KNOTGYM could inform the design of more capable robotic assistants, automated manufacturing systems, and even medical devices that require precise manipulation of flexible materials.

**Call to Action**

Researchers and practitioners interested in advancing the state of the art in visual reasoning, spatial manipulation, and embodied AI are encouraged to explore KNOTGYM. The environment, baseline implementations, and documentation are available at [https://github.com/lil-lab/knotgym](https://github.com/lil-lab/knotgym). For a comprehensive overview of the methodology, experimental results, and future directions, consult the full research paper at [https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.18028.pdf](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.18028.pdf).

End of News Article.

abstract: Streamlining constraints (or streamliners, for short) narrow the search space, enhancing the speed and feasibility of solving complex constraint satisfaction problems. Traditionally, streamliners were crafted manually or generated through systematically combined atomic constraints with high-effort offline testing. Our approach utilizes the generative capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs) to propose effective streamliners for problems specified in the MiniZinc constraint programming language and integrates feedback to the LLM with quick empirical tests for validation. Evaluated across seven diverse constraint satisfaction problems, our method achieves substantial runtime reductions. We compare the results to obfuscated and disguised variants of the problem to see whether the results depend on LLM memorization. We also analyze whether longer offline runs improve the quality of streamliners and whether the LLM can propose good combinations of streamliners.
introduction:
Introduction

Streamliners are certain constraints added to a constraint model to reduce the search space, thereby improving the feasibility and speed of finding solutions to complex constraint satisfac- tion problems. By incorporating domain-specific knowledge, streamliners can guide the con- straint solver, allowing it to bypass less promising areas of the search space. Gomes and Sell- mann [2004] introduced streamliners to speed up the constrained-based search for hard combina- torial design problems. Today, streamliners are a standard tool for speeding up constrained-based search. Streamliners are closely related to implied/redundant constraints, symmetry-breaking constraints, and dominance-breaking constraints; however, adding a streamliner may even cause the constraint model to become inconsistent.

Originally, streamliners were hand-crafted by researchers who used their theoretical insight to analyze the constrained model. However, progress has also been made on the automated gen- eration of streamliners [Spracklen et al., 2023] by systematically trying the effect of some atomic constraints, such as imposing specific constraints on integer and function domains, like enforcing odd or even values, monotonicity, and properties like commutativity, as well as facilitating spe- cific attributes in binary relations. These atomic restrictions are tested on thousands of problem instances, and those that show a good streamlining effect are systematically combined.

This paper proposes a different approach to automated streamliner generation that utilizes the generative capabilities (“creativity”) of Large Language Models (LLMs) to generate stream- liners, providing a neuro-symbolic prototype implementation StreamLLM, and rigorously tests

it on seven different constraint satisfaction problems using at least 310 CPU days1. Our ap- proach leverages the capabilities of LLMs to infer potentially effective streamliners, similar to an experienced researcher’s intuitive grasp of a problem. By integrating LLMs into the stream- liner generation process, we can potentially uncover unique and subtle patterns in the problem formulation and utilize them to speed up the solving process. To some extent, StreamLLM is closer related to a hand-crafted streamliner design by human experts rather than an automated bottom-up streamliner generation, the dominant strategy of the previous research [Spracklen et al., 2023].

Our system StreamLLM for streamliner generation combines queries to LLMs with quick em- pirical validation on small test instances solvable within seconds. The system generates effective constraints within minutes through engineered prompts and adaptive feedback based on stream- liner performance. This realtime approach allows streamliner benefits to be realized even for hard problem instances that would typically require hours to solve—any significant speedup can outweigh the brief time spent on streamliner generation.

We evaluate StreamLLM extensively across seven diverse constraint problems, including stan- dard benchmarks and a novel hypergraph coloring problem, using hundreds of instances spec- ified in MiniZinc. The problems are formulated in the MiniZinc constraint programming lan- guage [Nethercote et al., 2007]. We test StreamLLM with two state-of-the-art LLMs (GPT-4o and Claude 3.5 Sonnet), different prompting variants, and different strategies for combining adaptive prompting with test runs on easy instances. Our results demonstrate remarkable effectiveness, with some streamliners reducing solving time by up to 99%.

To investigate whether these improvements stem from memorization or genuine problem un- derstanding, we test StreamLLM on disguised and obfuscated variants of the problems. The system’s continued strong performance, particularly on problems with no known streamliners in the literature, suggests real analytical capabilities rather than mere pattern matching.

Beyond the realtime setting, we explore offline approaches with extended training periods and larger instance sets, achieving even better results in some cases. We also investigate StreamLLM’s ability to generate effective combinations of streamliners, finding that this often outperforms single constraints. Analysis of the generated streamliners reveals an interesting mix—some mirror expert-designed constraints while others take novel yet highly effective approaches.

Our experimental results suggest that LLM-generated streamliners can meaningfully reduce runtime across diverse constraint satisfaction problems with minimal problem-specific tuning, pointing to promising applications of AI assistance in constraint programming for computationally challenging problems.

Part of this work appeared in preliminary and shortened form at the 1st International Workshop on Neuro-Symbolic Software Engineering (NSE) [Voboril et al., 2025c].

The source code and benchmark instances can be found on Zenodo [Voboril et al., 2025b].

Headline: Large Language Models Accelerate Constraint Programming with Automated Streamliner Generation

News Article:

A new study demonstrates that Large Language Models (LLMs) can significantly enhance the efficiency of solving complex constraint satisfaction problems (CSPs) by automating the generation of streamliners—specialized constraints that narrow the search space and speed up solution discovery. The research, led by Voboril et al., introduces StreamLLM, a neuro-symbolic system that leverages LLMs to propose, test, and refine streamliners for problems specified in the MiniZinc constraint programming language. The results, published in a recent preprint, show that LLM-generated streamliners can reduce solving times by up to 99% across a diverse set of benchmark problems, marking a substantial advance in the intersection of artificial intelligence and constraint programming ([Voboril et al., 2025](http://arxiv.org/pdf/2408.10268v3.pdf)).

**Automating a Traditionally Manual Process**

Streamliners have long been recognized as a powerful tool in constraint programming, guiding solvers away from unpromising regions of the search space and thus improving computational feasibility. Traditionally, the design of streamliners has relied on expert intuition and deep domain knowledge, with researchers hand-crafting constraints based on theoretical insights. More recently, automated approaches have emerged, systematically generating and testing atomic constraints to identify effective streamliners. However, these methods often require extensive offline testing and are limited by the combinatorial explosion of possible constraint combinations.

StreamLLM represents a paradigm shift by harnessing the generative and analytical capabilities of state-of-the-art LLMs—specifically GPT-4o and Claude 3.5 Sonnet—to propose streamliners in a manner akin to human experts. The system operates in a feedback loop: LLMs generate candidate streamliners, which are then rapidly validated on small, easily solvable instances. Performance feedback is used to iteratively refine the prompts and constraints, enabling the discovery of effective streamliners within minutes.

**Broad Evaluation and Robust Performance**

The researchers evaluated StreamLLM on seven diverse CSPs, including standard benchmarks and a novel hypergraph coloring problem, using hundreds of instances formulated in MiniZinc. The system was tested with multiple LLMs, various prompting strategies, and both real-time and extended offline modes. In many cases, the LLM-generated streamliners achieved dramatic reductions in runtime, with some constraints slashing solution times by as much as 99%.

To address concerns about potential LLM memorization—where the model might simply recall known solutions rather than analyze the problem—the team tested StreamLLM on obfuscated and disguised variants of the benchmark problems. The system maintained strong performance even on these altered instances, particularly for problems with no known streamliners in the literature. This suggests that the LLMs are engaging in genuine problem analysis rather than relying solely on pattern matching or memorization.

**Insights into Streamliner Generation**

A notable finding of the study is that LLMs can generate not only effective individual streamliners but also powerful combinations of constraints. In several cases, these combinations outperformed single constraints, indicating that LLMs can synthesize complex, multi-faceted strategies for search space reduction. Analysis of the generated streamliners revealed a mix of familiar, expert-like constraints and novel approaches that had not previously been considered in the literature.

The system’s real-time feedback mechanism allows for rapid iteration, making it practical for use even on hard problem instances that would otherwise require hours to solve. Furthermore, the researchers explored offline approaches with longer training periods and larger instance sets, finding that extended runs could yield even higher-quality streamliners.

**Implications and Future Directions**

The success of StreamLLM highlights the growing potential of AI-assisted methods in constraint programming and combinatorial optimization. By automating the generation of streamliners, LLMs can reduce the need for extensive domain expertise and manual tuning, making advanced constraint-solving techniques more accessible to a broader range of practitioners. The approach also opens avenues for discovering new, previously unrecognized strategies for search space reduction.

While the study focuses on MiniZinc and a specific set of CSPs, the underlying methodology is broadly applicable and could be extended to other constraint programming languages and problem domains. The integration of LLMs into the constraint modeling workflow represents a promising step toward more intelligent, adaptive, and efficient problem-solving systems.

**Access the Full Research**

For researchers and practitioners interested in the technical details, experimental results, and broader implications of this work, the full research paper by Voboril et al. provides comprehensive insights and is available as a preprint. The source code and benchmark instances are also accessible for those wishing to replicate or build upon the findings. As AI continues to transform the landscape of computational problem-solving, this study offers a compelling example of how neuro-symbolic approaches can bridge the gap between human expertise and automated reasoning.

To explore the methodology, results, and future directions in depth, access the full research paper at [arXiv:2408.10268v3](http://arxiv.org/pdf/2408.10268v3.pdf).

End of News Article.

abstract: Habits are a fundamental driver of human behavior. However, prior research has largely studied habits in isolation. This study investigates spatial and smartphone habits—two cornerstone habits in contemporary society—in tandem, revealing the multiplicative ways in which habitual processes can underlie human behavior. We provide evidence that smartphone habits are stronger in habitually traveled and visited spaces. We leverage a custom-designed app that logged mobility and app use while prompting map-based questionnaires of participants’ trips for two weeks (N=419 participants; n=27,446,977 GPS and app logs; n=7,226 trip questionnaires). Overall, app habit strength predicted higher likelihood of app use (vs. non-use) and more app use (when used) on more habitually travelled routes and in more habitually visited destinations. The interaction between spatial and smartphone habits was most consistent for objective (vs. subjective) habit indicators. Social app habits were stronger and more context-independent than non-social app habits. Altogether, our findings reveal how distinct habitual processes can combine to shape daily behavior. Further, our method illustrates the potential of linking mobile sensing and surveying to measure habits in the real world.
introduction:
Habits are a direct and deep-rooted driver of human behavior1,2. By automating our cognition, habitual processes can make the world easier to navigate. Yet, they can also conflict with our goals3,4. For better and for worse, the degree to which behavior is performed habitually has powerful implications for how we engage with the world around us. Although people engage in a wide variety of habits in daily life, habits are typically studied in isolation5. By zooming out from this standard scope, we can reveal broader, multiplicative ways that habitual processes underlie real-world behavior across domains of everyday life.

Habitual behaviors are cued and performed automatically after being frequently rewarded in stable contexts2. In turn, the strength of a habit is indicated by the level of frequency (i.e., number of occurrences) and automaticity (i.e., lack of conscious processes)6. In contemporary society, spatial and smartphone habits act as cornerstone habits. Although a variety of factors can contribute to mobility choices and mobile phone use in a given situation, past research across disciplines indicates that habit is a leading predictor of both7,8. Critically, the interaction between these two types of habits is ideally suited for analyzing interactive habitual processes in daily life, as smartphone habits are uniquely activated across spaces9,10. Exploring their interactive relationship is also vital given the growing integration of mobility and technology: a convergence with implications for numerous facets of human behavior11–16.

Spatial habits are a prominent form of habitual behavior, as people often select and engage with their everyday spaces out of habit7,8. Scholars have extensively examined the role of habit in the selection of routes17–21, or how individuals travel from an origin to a destination via a particular mode of transportation (e.g., walking). Here, we focus on the role of habit in how people select routes as well as destinations, which together comprise the activity spaces that people inhabit in daily life22. Thus, spatial habits are comprised of places (i.e., destinations) and how individuals move between them (i.e., routes). Spatial habits can be cued by myriad factors (e.g., time, trip purpose)23. Research in cognitive geography has investigated spatial habits through the study of navigation, spatial cognition, and mental mapping. A notable example is anchor-point theory, which examines how people perceive and represent space through reference points like landmarks or neighborhoods24. Moreover, the centrality of spatial behavior in past habit research reflects the repetitive nature of daily mobility patterns identified in large-scale computational studies25,26. Spatial habits thereby have unique potential to operate as foundational habits: they determine the stable contexts that offer cues (or 'triggers') for other daily habits.

Smartphone habits are another dominant form of habitual behavior in modern society, as well as a heuristic case in habit research27. By bundling many possible behaviors (e.g., social media scrolling, podcast listening) into one handheld object, smartphones facilitate the development of a wide variety of activation cues (e.g., times, user activities), including personalized sets of cues tied to specific apps28. Accordingly, smartphone habits can be uniquely activated across a variety of spatial contexts9,10—challenging the presumed 'context-dependence' of traditional media habits and presumed role of location for cueing habits at-large2. However, some studies reveal that certain types of apps are also more likely to be used in certain types of places, as routes and destinations afford certain activities29,30. In this way, specific smartphone behaviors may develop implicit associations with specific types of spaces31,32—such as more habitually selected contexts33. In other words, spatial habits have the potential to make smartphone habits stronger.

To test this proposition, we examine whether more habitual apps are used disproportionately more in (1) habitually travelled routes and (2) habitually visited destinations. We test whether habitual apps can be interwoven with transportation habits by evaluating app use on routes. For example, app use and specific spaces may become embedded in scripts34: a habitual route (e.g., driving from home to work) can cue an app habit (e.g., listening to Spotify) at rehearsed points during the trip (e.g., traffic lights). These scripts may also be prompted by specific cues (e.g., time of day, social setting), thereby coupling spatial habits (e.g., the morning commute) and smartphone habits (e.g., listening to a news podcast) through sequences of cued actions. Additionally, we test the downstream influence of spatial habits on smartphone use by evaluating app use in destinations (e.g., the workplace). Since spaces chosen out of habit are more familiar35, habitually visited destinations may be more likely to contain familiar cues that activate app habits (e.g., checking a social media app while cleaning the kitchen). Altogether, we test whether app habits are activated more when individuals choose routes and destinations out of habit.

We further examine the robustness of the interaction between spatial and smartphone habits by exploring the distinctive role of social vs. non-social apps. Social apps provide a direct channel for social needs and norms to be activated habitually in daily life36. Based on the potency of social rewards in habit formation, along with the high proportion of social app usage in studies of overall smartphone usage37,38, social apps are expected to exhibit higher habit strength27,39. The theorized strength of social app habits may derive from their reliance on internal, non-spatial cues40. For example, people may habitually check social apps throughout the day in response to or in anticipation of new messages27,37. Thus, social app habits may be more likely to be context-independent: triggered across a variety of spatial contexts39.

Habits present a unique measurement challenge because they are triggered and performed with minimal conscious awareness throughout daily life41. Traditionally, habit research has centered on establishing the causal role of habit versus more conscious processes such as goals27. Here, moving beyond the question of whether habits or goals matter more, we focus on the real-world conditions—or contexts—under which habits become more likely to be activated. Accordingly, the current study sought to capture habits in the real world. By coupling GPS and app logs with map-based questionnaires, we collected objective and subjective indicators of spatial and smartphone habits and used those indicators to predict app use in context.

New Study Reveals How Spatial and Smartphone Habits Interact to Shape Daily Behavior

News Article:  
A recent study published in *Scientific Reports* has unveiled new insights into the interplay between spatial and smartphone habits, demonstrating that these two cornerstone behaviors in modern life do not operate in isolation but instead interact in ways that significantly influence daily routines. By leveraging a large-scale, real-world dataset, the research provides compelling evidence that habitual smartphone use is amplified in spaces that individuals frequent out of habit, offering a nuanced understanding of how technology and mobility converge to shape human behavior.

**Key Findings: The Multiplicative Nature of Habits**

The study, conducted by a multidisciplinary team, tracked 419 participants over two weeks using a custom-designed mobile application. This app collected over 27 million GPS and app usage logs, supplemented by more than 7,000 map-based trip questionnaires. The researchers found that the strength of an individual’s app habit—measured by frequency and automaticity—was a strong predictor of both the likelihood and intensity of app use, particularly on routes and in destinations that were habitually traveled or visited.

Crucially, the interaction between spatial and smartphone habits was most pronounced when measured using objective indicators, such as GPS logs, rather than subjective self-reports. This suggests that habitual behaviors are deeply embedded in daily routines, often occurring with minimal conscious awareness.

**Social Apps: Stronger and More Context-Independent**

One of the study’s notable contributions is its differentiation between social and non-social app habits. Social apps, such as messaging and social networking platforms, exhibited stronger habit strength and were more likely to be used across a variety of spatial contexts. This context-independence is attributed to the internal cues—such as anticipation of new messages or notifications—that drive social app use, as opposed to non-social apps, which are more closely tied to specific locations or activities.

The findings align with existing theories on the potency of social rewards in habit formation and the high prevalence of social app usage in overall smartphone behavior. The research suggests that while spatial context remains a significant cue for many smartphone habits, social app use is less constrained by location, reflecting the pervasive role of digital social interaction in contemporary life.

**Methodological Innovation: Linking Mobile Sensing and Surveying**

A key methodological advancement of the study is its integration of passive mobile sensing (GPS and app logs) with active surveying (map-based questionnaires). This approach allowed the researchers to capture both objective and subjective indicators of habit, providing a more comprehensive picture of how habits are formed and enacted in real-world settings.

By moving beyond traditional laboratory-based studies and self-reported measures, the research addresses a longstanding challenge in habit research: the difficulty of measuring behaviors that are often performed automatically and without conscious intent. The combination of large-scale, high-resolution data and context-aware surveys sets a new standard for studying habitual behavior in situ.

**Broader Implications: Understanding and Designing for Habit**

The study’s findings have significant implications for a range of fields, including behavioral science, human-computer interaction, urban planning, and digital health. Understanding how spatial and smartphone habits interact can inform the design of interventions aimed at promoting healthy behaviors, reducing digital distraction, or optimizing urban mobility.

For example, app developers and policymakers could leverage insights about the contexts in which smartphone habits are most likely to be activated to design more effective nudges or digital well-being tools. Similarly, urban planners might consider how habitual routes and destinations influence technology use when designing public spaces or transportation systems.

**A Call for Further Research**

While the study provides robust evidence of the interplay between spatial and smartphone habits, the authors note that further research is needed to explore the causal mechanisms underlying these interactions and to examine how they may vary across different populations and cultural contexts. The integration of mobile sensing and surveying represents a promising avenue for future studies seeking to unravel the complexities of habitual behavior in an increasingly digital and mobile world.

abstract:
Normalized mutual information is a biased measure for classification and community detection

Normalized mutual information is widely used as a similarity measure for evaluating the In this paper, we argue that results performance of clustering and classification algorithms. returned by the normalized mutual information are biased for two reasons: first, because they ignore the information content of the contingency table and, second, because their symmetric normalization introduces spurious dependence on algorithm output. We introduce a modified version of the mutual information that remedies both of these shortcomings. As a practical demonstration of the importance of using an unbiased measure, we perform extensive numer- ical tests on a basket of popular algorithms for network community detection and show that one’s conclusions about which algorithm is best are significantly affected by the biases in the traditional mutual information.
introduction:
1 Introduction

A common task in data analysis is the comparison of two different labelings of a set of objects. How well do demographics predict political affiliation? How accurately do blood tests predict clinical outcomes? These are questions about how similar one labeling of individuals/items/results is to another and they are commonly tackled using the information theoretic measure known as mutual information, in which an experimental or computational measure of some kind is compared against a ground truth.

Mutual information works by asking of how efficiently we can describe one labeling if we already know the other [1]. Specifically, it measures how much less information it takes to communicate the first labeling if we know the second versus if we do not. As an example, mutual information is commonly used in network science to evaluate the performance of algorithms for network commu- nity detection [2]. One takes a network whose community structure is already known and applies a community detection algorithm to it to infer the communities. Then one uses mutual information to compare the output of the algorithm to the known correct communities. Algorithms that con-

sistently achieve high mutual information scores are considered good. We will use this application as an illustrative example later in the paper.

Mutual information has a number of appealing properties as a tool for comparing labelings. It is invariant under permutations of the labels, so that, for example, two divisions of a network into communities that differ only in the numbering of the communities will be correctly identified as being the same. It also returns sensible results in cases where the number of distinct label values is not the same in the two labelings. However, the standard mutual information measure also has some significant shortcomings, and two in particular that we highlight in this paper. First, it has a bias towards labelings with too many distinct label values. For instance, a community detection algorithm that incorrectly divides networks into significantly more groups than are present in the ground truth can nonetheless achieve a high mutual information score. A number of approaches for correcting this flaw have been proposed. One can apply direct penalties for incorrect numbers of groups [3] or subtract correction terms based on the average mutual information over some ensemble of candidate labelings [4, 5] or on the statistics of the contingency table [6, 7]. For reasons we discuss shortly, we favor the latter approach, which leads to the measure known as the reduced mutual information.

The second drawback of the mutual information arises when the measure is normalized, as it commonly is to improve interpretability. The most popular normalization scheme creates a measure that runs between zero and one by dividing the mutual information by the arithmetic mean of the entropies of the two labelings being compared [8], although one can also normalize by the minimum, maximum, or geometric mean of the entropies. As we demonstrate in this paper, however, these normalizations introduce biases into the results by comparison with the unnormalized measure, because the normalization factor depends on the candidate labeling as well as the ground truth. This effect can be large enough to change scientific outcomes, and we provide examples of this phenomenon.

In order to avoid this latter bias, while still retaining the interpretability of a normalized mutual information measure, we favor normalizing by the entropy of the ground-truth labeling alone. This removes the source of bias but introduces an asymmetry in the normalization. At first sight this asymmetry may seem undesirable, and previous authors have gone to some lengths to avoid it. Here, however, we argue that it is not only justified but actually desirable, for several reasons. First, many of the classification problems we consider are unaffected by the asymmetry, since they involve the comparison of one or more candidate labelings against a single, unchanging ground truth. Moreover, by contrast with the multitude of possible symmetric normalizations, the asymmetric measure we propose is the unique way to normalize the mutual information without introducing biases due to the normalization itself.

More broadly, the asymmetric measure is more informative than the conventional symmetric one. Consider for instance the common situation where the groups or communities in one labeling are a subdivision of those in the other. We might for example label individuals by the country they live in on the one hand and by the town or city on the other. Then the more detailed labeling tells us everything there is to know about the coarser one, but the reverse is not true. Telling you the city fixes the country, but not vice versa. Thus one could argue that the mutual information between the two eshould be asymmetric, and this type of asymmetry will be a key feature of the measures we study.

Both drawbacks of the standard mutual information—bias towards too many groups and depen- dence of the normalization on the candidate labeling—can be addressed simultaneously by using an asymmetric normalized reduced mutual information as defined in this paper. In support of this approach we present an extensive comparison of the performance of this and other variants of the mutual information in network community detection tasks, generating a large number of random

test networks with known community structure and a variety of structural parameters, and then attempting to recover the communities using popular community detection algorithms. Within this framework, we find that conclusions about which algorithms perform best are significantly impacted by the choice of mutual information measure, and specifically that traditional measures erroneously favor algorithms that find too many communities, but our proposed measure does not. Code im- plementing our measure can be found at https://github.com/maxjerdee/clustering-mi.

Headline:  
**New Study Reveals Biases in Normalized Mutual Information for Clustering and Community Detection**

News Article:  
A recent study has cast new light on the reliability of normalized mutual information (NMI), a widely used metric for evaluating clustering and classification algorithms in computer science. The research, available as a preprint on arXiv, demonstrates that NMI—long considered a gold standard for comparing algorithmic outputs to ground truth—can introduce significant biases that may distort scientific conclusions, particularly in the context of network community detection.

**Key Findings and Impact**

The authors identify two primary sources of bias in traditional NMI. First, NMI tends to favor labelings with an excessive number of groups, potentially rewarding algorithms that over-partition data. Second, the symmetric normalization commonly used in NMI calculations introduces a spurious dependence on the candidate algorithm’s output, rather than solely on the ground truth. These biases, the study argues, can be substantial enough to alter which algorithms are deemed most effective in comparative studies.

To address these issues, the researchers propose a modified version of mutual information—an asymmetric normalized reduced mutual information—that corrects both sources of bias. Their extensive numerical experiments, conducted on a suite of popular community detection algorithms and synthetic networks with known structures, reveal that the choice of mutual information measure can significantly affect which algorithms appear to perform best. Notably, traditional NMI often erroneously favors algorithms that identify too many communities, while the new measure provides a more accurate assessment.

**Understanding Mutual Information in Algorithm Evaluation**

Mutual information (MI) is a foundational concept in information theory, quantifying how much knowing one labeling of a dataset reduces uncertainty about another. In practical terms, MI is used to compare the output of clustering or classification algorithms against a reference or ground truth labeling. Its invariance to label permutations and ability to handle differing numbers of groups make it attractive for a range of applications, from political science to medical diagnostics and, notably, network science.

However, as the study highlights, MI is not without its flaws. When used in its standard form, MI can be biased towards solutions that overfit by creating too many clusters. Various correction methods have been proposed, including penalizing incorrect group counts or adjusting for the expected MI over random labelings. The reduced mutual information approach, which the authors favor, subtracts a correction term based on the statistics of the contingency table—a matrix summarizing the overlap between two labelings.

**The Problem with Symmetric Normalization**

To enhance interpretability, MI is often normalized to produce a score between zero and one. The most common approach divides MI by the arithmetic mean of the entropies of the two labelings. While this symmetric normalization is intuitive, the study demonstrates that it introduces a problematic dependence on the candidate labeling. This means that the normalization factor itself can vary based on the algorithm’s output, rather than being anchored to the ground truth, leading to potentially misleading comparisons.

The authors advocate for an asymmetric normalization, using only the entropy of the ground-truth labeling as the normalization factor. While this breaks the symmetry of the measure, the researchers argue that such asymmetry is not only justified but desirable. In many real-world scenarios, one labeling (such as a detailed city-level classification) contains strictly more information than another (such as a country-level classification). The asymmetric measure more accurately reflects these relationships and avoids the biases introduced by symmetric normalization.

**Empirical Validation and Broader Implications**

To validate their approach, the researchers conducted large-scale experiments using synthetic networks with known community structures. They applied several popular community detection algorithms and compared their outputs using both traditional and modified mutual information measures. The results were striking: conclusions about algorithm performance were highly sensitive to the choice of measure, with traditional NMI often favoring algorithms that over-partitioned the data.

The implications of these findings are significant for the broader computer science community. As clustering and community detection are foundational tasks in fields ranging from bioinformatics to social network analysis, the reliability of evaluation metrics is paramount. The study’s results suggest that researchers and practitioners should exercise caution when interpreting NMI scores and consider adopting the proposed asymmetric normalized reduced mutual information for more accurate assessments.

**Looking Forward**

The authors have made their implementation of the new measure publicly available, encouraging further exploration and adoption by the research community. As the field continues to evolve, the careful selection and understanding of evaluation metrics will remain critical for advancing algorithmic development and ensuring robust scientific conclusions.

For those interested in a deeper dive into the methodology, empirical results, and theoretical underpinnings of these findings, the full research paper is accessible at [arXiv:2307.01282v3](http://arxiv.org/pdf/2307.01282v3.pdf). Researchers and practitioners are encouraged to consult the original work for comprehensive details and to consider the implications of metric selection in their own studies.

End of News Article.

abstract: Abstract While test-time reasoning enables language models (LMs) to tackle com- plex tasks, searching or planning in natural language can be slow, costly, and error-prone. But even when LMs struggle to emulate the precise rea- soning steps needed to solve a problem, they often excel at describing its abstract structure—both how to verify solutions and how to search for them. This paper introduces DISCIPL, a method for “self-steering” LMs where a Planner model generates a task-specific inference program that is executed by a population of Follower models. Our approach equips LMs with the abil- ity to write recursive search procedures that guide LM inference, enabling new forms of verifiable and efficient reasoning. When instantiated with a small Follower (e.g., Llama-3.2-1B or Qwen3-1.7B), DISCIPL matches (and sometimes outperforms) much larger models, including GPT-4o and o1, on challenging constrained generation tasks. Our work opens up a design space of highly-parallelized Monte Carlo inference strategies that outperform standard best-of-N sampling, require no finetuning, and can be implemented automatically by existing LMs.
introduction:
Introduction

Even as language models (LMs) are becoming increasingly proficient at reasoning tasks, progress has been “jagged” (Karpathy, 2024; Roose, 2025): today’s frontier models surpass experts at science and math reasoning (e.g., Hendrycks et al., 2021; Rein et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2024b) yet still routinely struggle with counting, arithmetic, tic-tac-toe, metered po- etry, and other intuitively simple tasks (Ball et al., 2024; McCoy et al., 2023; Xu & Ma, 2024). For example, even very capable LMs have difficulty writing a coherent sentence under the constraints in Fig. 1, which are manageable for most proficient English speakers.

There is a growing consensus that many problems require a more deliberate, effortful style of thinking (Kahneman, 2011). However, an open question is how to structure inference so as to best leverage available computational resources. One popular approach performs in-context reasoning via serialized chain-of-thought (DeepSeek-AI et al., 2025; OpenAI, 2024b). While highly flexible, reasoning via autoregressive generation is costly, slow, and can still produce unreliable outputs. On the other hand, structured inference methods like tree search (Silver et al., 2016; Yao et al., 2023) and sequential Monte Carlo (Lew et al., 2023; Loula et al., 2025; Zhao et al., 2024) attain better parallelism and efficiency by coordinating test-time computation via external algorithms. However, these methods require significant hand-engineering and rely on pre-defined scorers or verifiers, limiting their applicability.

In this work, we propose a new meta-reasoning framework called DISCIPL in which lan- guage models themselves drive the decisions for how to structure inference-time computa- tion. In our approach, a Planner LM generates an ad-hoc problem specification (encoding its understanding of the task requirements) and inference procedure (encoding its plan for how to solve the task). Importantly, the specification and plan are implemented as inference programs that invoke Follower LMs, either generatively or as likelihood evaluators. By decomposing reasoning into planning and execution, our architecture preserves flexibility while enabling orchestration of highly efficient, parallel search patterns.
